context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly kind of stuck on the SWOT analysis for my city case study. I get the basic idea of strengths/weaknesses/opportunities/threats, but once I try to connect it to the spider diagram, power/actor map, and stressors, it all starts feeling too broad. Could you just explain how all those pieces are supposed to fit together?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—treat each earlier assignment as a different lens on the same city.

Try this order:
1. Spider Diagram = where performance is strong vs weak across the 6 pathways.
2. Power/Actor Map = who can improve or block those weak areas.
3. Stressors = what climate pressures make those weaknesses more urgent.
4. SWOT = sort all that into internal vs external factors.

A useful check:
- Strengths/Weaknesses = internal to the city’s current systems, capacity, policies, infrastructure, governance.
- Opportunities/Threats = external conditions the city could leverage or must respond to.

So the pieces fit like this: “Which pathways are weak, who has power over them, and how do the city’s stressors test them?”

What is one pathway in your spider diagram that scored relatively low for your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep this bite-sized and use guided discovery rather than doing the assignment for the student. I should clarify the relationship among the required course tools without filling in the SWOT table for them. To move them toward solving the task, I will frame the assignment as a sequence: diagnose weak pathways with the Spider Diagram, identify who can change them with the Power/Actor Map, pressure-test with stressors, and then sort findings into internal/external SWOT categories. I will ask one focused question at the end so the student must apply the structure to their own city.

exchanges.student: I think governance/civic engagement was one of the weaker ones, but I’m not totally sure because a bunch of the pathways seem connected. That framing helped a little, but I’m still tempted to just write a big explanation for the whole city instead of separating each pathway. Is it okay if I think about it more like one overall city SWOT first?
exchanges.tutor:
You can use an overall city SWOT only as a rough brainstorm—but don’t stop there. The assignment explicitly wants the table filled out for each pathway.

Since the pathways are connected, ask: in governance/civic engagement, what is actually internal to city decision-making, and what is coming from outside pressure or outside support?

Start with just one cell: for that pathway, what would count as one internal strength and one internal weakness in your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's uncertainty, but steer them back to the assignment requirement: the SWOT table is for each pathway. I should not approve a shortcut that weakens alignment with the prompt. Using Socratic guidance, I will let them use an overall city SWOT only as a brainstorming step, then push them to test one pathway with a few targeted questions. This keeps the response concise and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get lost, because everything feels arguable either way. Like public participation could be a strength if the city has meetings, but a weakness if those meetings don’t really change decisions. That didn’t totally solve it for me, so maybe I’m thinking about the categories wrong—could you give me a fuller example of how to classify a whole pathway?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re not stuck on SWOT—you’re stuck on evidence quality. Same fact can look positive or negative depending on performance.

Use this test:
- If the city already has the thing and it helps climate action in practice → strength.
- If the city has it, but it functions poorly or unevenly → weakness.
- If it does not yet exist locally but outside conditions could enable it → opportunity.
- If outside conditions could undermine progress → threat.

So for participation: don’t ask “are there meetings?” Ask “do they materially shape climate decisions?”

Pick one concrete governance fact from your city, and classify it using that test.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not provide a filled example that risks becoming a partial solution. Instead, I will tighten the student's classification rule and use guided discovery with a neutral template. Since they are stuck on ambiguity, I can be slightly less Socratic by giving a decision test for classification without supplying city-specific content. I will keep it brief and ask them to apply the test.

exchanges.student: I guess one fact is that the city has public consultation processes, but I don’t know how much influence they actually have, and now I feel like I need to research every single claim before I can write anything. I’m probably overcomplicating it, but can you just show me what a completed mini-SWOT for governance/civic engagement would look like so I can copy the logic?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to fill in a mini-SWOT for you. My role is to help you think it through, not supply content you can copy.

You do not need perfect proof for every line. Use a simple rule:
- formal process exists → possible strength
- weak influence, low turnout, or inequitable access → weakness
- new funding/laws/partnerships that could improve participation → opportunity
- political backlash, mistrust, budget cuts, or crisis overload → threat

Now do one honest draft: give me 1 possible strength and 1 possible weakness for governance/civic engagement in your city, even if tentative.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a completed mini-SWOT, which would violate the rule against providing partial solutions. I should refuse that directly, explain my role, and instead give a minimal analytic structure they can use themselves. Because they are overcomplicating and stuck, I can be slightly less Socratic by giving a decision process and evidence threshold, then ask for a short attempt. This keeps the interaction on the assignment and moves them toward producing their own content.

exchanges.student: Okay, tentative then: a strength might be that there’s at least some formal channel for public input, and a weakness might be that participation is uneven and maybe not very influential. That still feels super generic, though, and I’m worried the whole assignment will sound like vague filler unless I make every pathway really detailed.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a workable start, but yes—right now it’s too generic.

To stop filler, attach each point to one concrete indicator:
- a policy, plan, office, or recurring process
- a participation pattern or equity gap
- a recent controversy or decision outcome
- a data point from your earlier assignments

Don’t make every pathway equally detailed at first. Make each entry specific.

For your governance weakness, what evidence would show “uneven and not influential”—who is excluded, or what decision ignored public input?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's draft without overpraising, point out why it is currently too generic, and guide them to make it concrete using evidence prompts tied to the assignment. I will not supply examples for their city or write language for them. I will keep it short and ask for one pathway-specific refinement to move them toward a usable SWOT entry.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure, and that didn’t really work, so I’m trying a different angle: maybe I should just choose the two best opportunities first and then backfill the SWOT around them? I feel like if I start from the “promising opportunities” part, the rest might be easier.
exchanges.tutor:
You can use that as a temporary shortcut, but don’t reverse the assignment completely. The two opportunities are supposed to emerge from the SWOT, not replace it.

Better approach: pick 1 tentative opportunity, then test it against three things:
- which weak pathway it improves
- which actors could actually move it
- which stressor it helps address

If it fails one of those, it’s probably not one of your best two.

What is one opportunity you’re considering for your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is proposing a potentially useful but risky shortcut. I should redirect without fully shutting it down: opportunities should emerge from the SWOT, but they can use tentative opportunities as a hypothesis. I will keep them aligned with the assignment and ask a focused question that links opportunities to existing weaknesses, actors, and stressors.

exchanges.student: Maybe expanding community-based climate planning or resilience hubs, because that seems to connect engagement and adaptation. I’m still fuzzy on whether that counts as an “opportunity” versus a policy recommendation, and I kind of want a bigger-picture explanation of how to tell those apart.
exchanges.tutor:
Good question. They are related, but not the same.

Think of it this way:
- Opportunity = a favorable opening or condition the city could leverage.
- Policy recommendation = the action you later argue the city should take because of that opening.

So ask:
- What makes this possible now? That’s the opportunity.
- What should the city do about it? That’s the recommendation.

For your idea, what is the enabling condition: funding, partnerships, community demand, state/federal policy, recent disasters, existing networks, something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will clarify the conceptual distinction between an opportunity and a policy recommendation without drafting content for the student. Since the student asks for bigger-picture explanation, I can provide a compact framework, still using guided discovery. I will then ask them to classify their own idea using the framework so they actively participate.

exchanges.student: Maybe existing community organizations plus recent heat or flooding problems make people pay more attention, so that creates an opening. I think I sort of get it, but I’m still feeling like I need a more complete model in my head of the whole assignment before I can write. Could you walk through the overall structure one more time, kind of start to finish?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Here’s the full logic, start to finish:

1. Re-check Spider Diagram
- Which pathways are strongest?
- Which need the most work?

2. Re-check Power/Actor Map
- Who has power in those weaker pathways?
- Who is missing or excluded?

3. Re-check Stressors
- Which climate pressures expose the city’s weak points?

4. Build SWOT pathway by pathway
For each pathway, sort your evidence into:
- Strengths = internal assets already helping
- Weaknesses = internal limits/gaps already hurting
- Opportunities = external openings the city could leverage
- Threats = external pressures/risks that could block progress

5. Look across the table
- Which weaknesses show up repeatedly?
- Which opportunities build on real strengths and fit the actor landscape?

6. Choose your best two opportunities
They should:
- help weak pathways
- be feasible given actors/power
- respond to stressors

7. Later, turn one of those into a policy recommendation
That comes after the SWOT logic, not before.

Don’t overbuild a theory of the whole paper before writing. Start one pathway at a time.

Which pathway are you going to draft first: governance/civic engagement, or the adaptation-related one connected to resilience hubs?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a complete model of the assignment. I should provide a concise end-to-end process aligned to the prompt, without filling in content. I will keep it bite-sized and organized, showing workflow from prior assignments to SWOT to selecting two opportunities. To preserve guided discovery, I will end by asking them to choose their first step.

exchanges.student: I’ll probably start with the adaptation one since it feels easier, but I still kind of want to write a broad city-level paragraph first so I don’t lose the big picture. That summary approach hasn’t really worked for me yet, so I may switch again after I try a draft.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re circling the planning step. Bigger picture: this assignment rewards pathway-specific evidence, not a polished city-wide intro.

If a broad paragraph helps you think, fine—but don’t hide in it. Move on quickly.

Next step: draft just the adaptation pathway with 1 item in each SWOT box, tied to one stressor and one actor. Send that rough attempt if you want feedback.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wavering and showing a redundant pattern of wanting a broad overview instead of drafting pathway-specific content. Per the rules, I should point out the redundancy, refocus on the bigger picture of the assignment, and ask them to produce a concrete written attempt rather than continue abstract discussion. I will keep it concise, not provide wording, and ask for a small next step tied to the adaptation pathway.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try that, but I’m still worried I’m forcing things into boxes. If I draft one item in each box for adaptation, can you then help me check whether I’m mixing up internal and external factors?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s a good use of tutoring. I can help you test your classifications.

Quick rule:
- Internal = the city’s own systems, policies, capacity, infrastructure, governance
- External = conditions outside the city’s direct control that shape its options

Draft your 4 adaptation items first. For each one, ask: “Does the city already possess/control this, or is this something around the city that it must leverage or respond to?”

Send your draft when ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should reassure the student that checking classification is appropriate tutoring support, without promising to solve it for them. I will encourage a concrete draft and give a minimal internal/external test they can apply. This keeps the response brief, Socratic, and aligned with the assignment.
